name: LightGBM
summary: LightGBM uses histogram-based gradient boosting with leaf-wise tree growth, reducing memory usage and training time on large datasets by splitting the leaf with the maximum delta loss rather than growing level by level. It combines gradient-based one-side sampling and exclusive feature bundling to achieve state-of-the-art speed on tabular data.
use_when: Large tabular datasets with millions of rows and hundreds of features. Production pipelines requiring fast training cycles and low memory usage. Datasets containing high-cardinality categorical features that can be passed natively without manual encoding.
avoid_when: Small datasets with fewer than 10,000 samples where aggressive leaf-wise growth can overfit. Applications requiring deterministic results across different hardware or library versions. When exact split finding is needed rather than histogram-based approximation.
pros: Fastest training speed among gradient boosting libraries on large tabular data due to histogram-based binning and leaf-wise growth
Native categorical feature support eliminates the need for one-hot encoding or manual ordinal encoding
Low memory footprint via histogram-based binning, exclusive feature bundling, and gradient-based one-side sampling
Supports distributed learning and GPU acceleration for training on massive datasets across multiple machines
Efficient handling of missing values without requiring explicit imputation or preprocessing
cons: Overfitting risk on small datasets due to aggressive leaf-wise growth that can create overly deep trees
Leaf-wise tree structure is harder to tune than level-wise XGBoost because num_leaves and max_depth interact non-linearly
Histogram binning can introduce approximation errors on features with extreme outliers or very sparse distributions
Fewer built-in regularization options compared to XGBoost, making it more reliant on early stopping and row sampling

key_hyperparams.name: n_estimators
key_hyperparams.note: Number of boosting iterations. Use 100-1000 with early stopping. LightGBM often needs fewer trees than XGBoost to achieve equivalent accuracy because leaf-wise growth is more expressive.

key_hyperparams.name: num_leaves
key_hyperparams.note: Maximum number of leaves in one tree. Controls model complexity. Set to 20-50 for small datasets, up to 150 for large datasets. Keep num_leaves well below 2^(max_depth) to avoid overfitting.

key_hyperparams.name: learning_rate
key_hyperparams.note: Shrinkage rate applied to each tree's contribution. Lower to 0.05-0.1 and increase n_estimators for better regularization. Default 0.1 works well when paired with early stopping.

key_hyperparams.name: max_depth
key_hyperparams.note: Maximum tree depth. -1 means no limit. Set to 6-12 to constrain leaf-wise growth and prevent overfitting on small datasets, especially when num_leaves is large.

key_hyperparams.name: min_child_samples
key_hyperparams.note: Minimum number of data samples required in a leaf. Increase to 50-100 on noisy datasets to reduce overfitting on outlier nodes and improve generalization.

key_hyperparams.name: feature_fraction
key_hyperparams.note: Fraction of features selected per tree. Set to 0.8-0.9 to introduce feature bagging and reduce overfitting on high-dimensional data without sacrificing too much speed.

quick_start:
from lightgbm import LGBMClassifier

clf = LGBMClassifier(
    n_estimators=500,
    num_leaves=31,
    learning_rate=0.1,
    max_depth=-1,
    min_child_samples=20,
    feature_fraction=0.8,
    verbose=-1
)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
preds = clf.predict(X_test)
decision_notes: Choose LightGBM for fastest training on large tabular datasets. Use XGBoost for better regularization. Use Random Forest for simpler baseline.
